coredata.dc:title: Waste to functions: Construction of a novel solar-thermal conversion material based on sludge-derived carbon dots via Ca-in-situ-functionalization for efficient solar-driven water evaporation
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Yutong
Zheng, Jingjing
Wei, Lecheng
Zhu, Liang
coredata.dc:description: Solar interface water evaporator (SIWE) based on waste-derived carbon dots (CDs) are highly promising for large-scale production applications in the future, due to their low-cost, eco-friendly, and tunable structure and properties. However, there are still obstacles, including limited performance, blind selection of waste materials, and unknown underlying mechanisms between waste properties and the physicochemical properties of derived CDs. In this study, a novel CDs-based wooden SIWE derived from paper mill sludge (PMS) was constructed (called ESCDs-wood). Under 1 sun, ESCDs rapidly heated up from 15.6 °C to 53.5 °C and reached stability within 10 mins. The solar-thermal conversion efficiency of ESCDs was as high as 137.58 %, and the ESCDs-wood achieved a high evaporation rate of 2.94 kg m-2h−1. Moreover, the ESCDs-wood cost about $9 to complete 1 m2 evaporation. Meanwhile, the ESCDs-wood exhibited long-term stability, after 20 cycles, its solar-thermal conversion efficiency and water evaporation rate remained at 84.21 % and 85.03 % of the initial test, respectively. Moreover, the Ca-doping was found to be a key factor in effectively enhancing the solar-thermal conversion and the thermal stability of ESCDs, and the exact role of Ca-doping on the solar-thermal conversion performance enhancement of CDs was validated and revealed. Therefore, this study not only constructed a cost-effectiveness, high performance, sustainability, chemical-free, and eco-friendly SIWE, but also innovatively proposed the functionalization mechanism of Ca-doping for CDs and thus provided a new sight for the targeted regulation and design of CDs.

head.ce:title: Waste to functions: Construction of a novel solar-thermal conversion material based on sludge-derived carbon dots via Ca-in-situ-functionalization for efficient solar-driven water evaporation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solar interface water evaporator (SIWE) based on waste-derived carbon dots (CDs) are highly promising for large-scale production applications in the future, due to their low-cost, eco-friendly, and tunable structure and properties. However, there are still obstacles, including limited performance, blind selection of waste materials, and unknown underlying mechanisms between waste properties and the physicochemical properties of derived CDs. In this study, a novel CDs-based wooden SIWE derived from paper mill sludge (PMS) was constructed (called ESCDs-wood). Under 1 sun, ESCDs rapidly heated up from 15.6 °C to 53.5 °C and reached stability within 10 mins. The solar-thermal conversion efficiency of ESCDs was as high as 137.58 %, and the ESCDs-wood achieved a high evaporation rate of 2.94 kg m-2h−1. Moreover, the ESCDs-wood cost about $9 to complete 1 m2 evaporation. Meanwhile, the ESCDs-wood exhibited long-term stability, after 20 cycles, its solar-thermal conversion efficiency and water evaporation rate remained at 84.21 % and 85.03 % of the initial test, respectively. Moreover, the Ca-doping was found to be a key factor in effectively enhancing the solar-thermal conversion and the thermal stability of ESCDs, and the exact role of Ca-doping on the solar-thermal conversion performance enhancement of CDs was validated and revealed. Therefore, this study not only constructed a cost-effectiveness, high performance, sustainability, chemical-free, and eco-friendly SIWE, but also innovatively proposed the functionalization mechanism of Ca-doping for CDs and thus provided a new sight for the targeted regulation and design of CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Growing energy needs and depleting fossil energy sources are leading to a rising demand for renewable energy conversion and storage [1]. Solar-thermal energy conversion is a highly promising form of sustainable energy conversion [2], and it is also a core process supporting diverse application fields [3]. Among them, solar interface water evaporation (SIWE) is a sustainable and low-energy consumption technology that utilizes solar-thermal conversion to produce fresh water, which is expected to alleviate the global freshwater crisis [4]. Effective SIWE systems rely on the exploration for advanced solar-thermal conversion materials (STCMs) that are high-performance, low-cost, eco-friendly, and sustainability [5]. Among various materials, carbon-based nanomaterials have become an advantageous functional STCMs for SIWE due to their easy availability, superior solar-thermal conversion, and diverse surface physicochemical properties [6,7]. Recently, carbon dots (CDs), as a type of carbon nanomaterials, have exhibited advantages in solar-thermal conversion for SIWE due to its tunable structure, surface functionalization, broad range of light absorption, and easy to be prepared [8]. In previous studies, different kinds of CDs have been utilized for SIWE systems. In 2019, Singh et al. used phenylenediamine to prepare CDs, and constructed a CDs-hydrogel SIWE system that achieved a solar-thermal conversion efficiency of 89.0 % as well as a water evaporation rate of 1.4 kg m-2h−1 [9]. In the same year, Hou et al. prepared CDs with Coal pitch and constructed a SIWE system for CDs-delignified wood, achieving a significant breakthrough in solar-thermal conversion efficiency (92.5 %) as well as water evaporation rate (2.3 kg m-2h−1) [10]. However, most of the solar-thermal conversion efficiencies of CDs were less than 100 % and the water evaporation rates achieved by SIWE systems with CDs almost all ranged between 1.4 and 2.6 kg m-2h−1 [11]. Therefore, CDs for SIWE systems still suffer from barriers such as limited conversion and evaporation efficiency, unknown ecotoxicity, complex treatment, and inability to chemical-free [12]. Therefore, it is imperative to design high conversion efficiency, chemical-free, eco-friendly, and scalable CDs for solar-thermal conversion from low-cost, green, and renewable resources [13].

Waste/biomass is considered as an advantageous carbon precursor for the preparation of functional CDs due to its widely available, low-cost, environmentally friendly and sustainable [5]. Notably, waste/biomass always contained abundant heteroatoms and well-ordered natural structures, which can realize self-doping during the preparations, [14], thereby endowing its derived CDs with diverse potential characteristics [15]. Therefore, utilizing waste/biomass derived carbon dots for photothermal conversion can not only make the SIWE system more sustainable, low-cost, and environmentally friendly, but also have the potential to break through the current conversion efficiency by selecting appropriate waste/biomass raw materials and regulating strategy. However, the following obstacles to constructing waste/biomass derived CDs for solar thermal energy conversion still exist: (1) Currently, there are fewer wastes derived CDs that have the potential for efficient solar-thermal conversion [16]. (2) the underlying mechanism between the waste/biomass raw materials and the physicochemical properties of their derived CDs, especially in terms of heteroatom doping, is difficult to explain and has not yet been explored [1]. (3) the selection of waste materials is blind, and it is inability to design an effective utilization strategy tailored to the characteristics of the raw material themselves [17]. (4) No study has yet elucidated which specific function a particular modification strategy would provide for CDs [18]. Therefore, if waste/biomass, introduced heteroatom doping, physicochemical properties of CDs, and application advantages of solar-thermal conversion can be correlated, it will be of great significance for the directed regulation and synthesis of functional CDs in solar-thermal conversion.

Paper mill sludge (PMS) is a common industrial waste from the paper industry. In China, approximately 129 million tons of paper mill sludge are produced every year [19]. The high calcium (Ca) content (12.7 %-50 %) is the most characteristic feature of PMS [20,21], making it a “Ca resource library”. Therefore, recycling and utilizing resources based on the high-Ca properties of PMS is one of the most intelligent strategies to maximize the properties of the raw material. In our previous study, Ca-N self-doped CDs were successfully synthesized from PMS, and we found that Ca-doping enhanced the fluorescence performance of CDs and most likely also enhanced the photothermal properties of CDs [22]. Therefore, this study continued to investigate the role of Ca-oping in enhancing the photothermal performance of CDs and the related mechanisms.

In order to further improve the performance of the SIWE system, to reduce the cost of the system, to minimize the environmental impact of the system, and to reveal the role of Ca-doping for CDs, a novel sustainable wooden-evaporator derived from waste, with high performance, chemical-free in the whole process, and high economic efficiency was proposed in this study. CDs derived from paper mill excess sludge (ESCDs) were selected to construct solar-thermal conversion layer. The solar-thermal conversion performance of ESCDs and the water evaporation performance of the ESCDs-based evaporator and its economic feasibility were investigated. More importantly, the solar-thermal conversion mechanism of ESCD was elucidated, and the effect of Ca-doping on the solar-thermal conversion performance of CDs was investigated.

In contrast to previous studies, this study significantly improved the cost-effectiveness, sustainability and environmental friendliness of the SIWE system, and also achieved higher solar-thermal conversion efficiency and water evaporation efficiency. Meanwhile, for the first time, the high-Ca properties of PMS, Ca-doping of ESCDs, and efficient solar-thermal conversion of ESCDs were correlated. In addition, the exact role of calcium doping on the solar-thermal conversion performance enhancement of CDs was revealed for the first time. Therefore, this study broke the knowledge gap of the underlying mechanism between the waste raw materials and the physicochemical properties of their derived CDs, revealed the specific role of heteroatom doping, and provided a new strategy for the efficient utilization of waste and targeted regulatory modification of CDs.

Citric acid, urea, and ethylenediamine were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. Balsa wood was purchased from Kiko Balsa Co., Ltd. The paper mill excess sludge was obtained from a paper mill in Jiaxing, China. The municipal sludge was obtained from an urban sewage treatment plant in Hangzhou, China.
The above sludge and the municipal sludge were firstly dried at 105 °C, then grind into powder and screened (40-mesh) for use.

Paper mill excess sludge-derived CDs (ESCDs): The specific synthesis method referred to our group's previous research [22]. As shown in Fig. 1 , Paper mill sludge was mixed with deionized water (the solid–liquid ratio was 2:10) and then magnetically stirred until homogeneous. The mixture was put into a 150-mL autoclave for hydrothermal reaction (200 °C, 12 h). The obtained solution was firstly centrifuged (20,000 × g, 1 h) and filtered (0.22 μm), then dried overnight at 80 °C, to obtain the ESCDs for use. At this time, the dried ESCDs were gel-like and easy to coat (Fig. 1). After coating on the surface of the carrier and then drying, the ESCDs were then in the form of a dense membrane-like on the surface of the carrier and showed a certain gloss (Fig. 1).

Citric acid and urea-derived CDs (CD2): Citric acid and urea were mixed in 100 mL deionized water at a molar ratio of 1:1 for the hydrothermal reaction (200 °C, 4 h). The subsequent steps were the same as above.

Citric acid and ethylenediamine-derived CDs (CD3): Citric acid and ethylenediamine were mixed in 100 mL deionized water at a molar ratio of 1:1 for the hydrothermal reaction (200 °C, 4 h). The subsequent steps were the same as above.

Preparation of ESCDs-coated wood: after drying, the ESCDs, CD2 and CD3 were coated on the top of balsa wood (length
×
width of 1.25
×
1.25 cm) with a thickness of about 2 mm, and placed in an oven at 80 °C for drying.

Interfacial water evaporator: the CDs (ESCDs, CD2, CD3)-wood was supported by a 1 cm-thick polystyrene foam, the top (except for the wood area) was covered with a layer of tinfoil for insulation. The entire evaporation system was conducted in a 50 mL beaker (Fig. 1).

The morphology of the ESCDs-wood was recorded by scanning electron microscopy (Hitachi, TM-1000 h). UV–visible-NIR (200 nm-2500 nm) diffuse reflectance spectrum was measured by UV-3600 Spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU, Japan). The XPS spectrum was measured by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha). Fourier transform infrared spectrum was obtained by FTIR spectroscopy (Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS20). Contact angles were measured by Optical Contact Angle Measuring Instrument (Theta Lite, Finland) with 3.58 μL droplets. Deionized water was used to test at room temperature [23,24].

The light source was simulated by xenon lamp (CEL-HXF300, Au-light). The light intensity was monitored by a light power meter (CEL-NP2000, Au-light). Mass were recorded by electronic balance (XY1000-2C, Longreen). Temperature were recorded by IR camera (H21PRO, HIKVISION).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Ca-doping on solar-thermal conversion
ce:section.ce:para: 20.0 g of municipal excess sludge (low to no calcium) was mixed with deionized water at the solid–liquid ratio (2:10), the 0 g, 5 g, 10 g, 15 g, 20 g of calcium chloride were added respectively, (Calcium chloride to residual sludge mass ratios of 20:0, 4:1, 2:1, 4:3, and 1:1, respectively) then magnetically stirred until homogeneous. The mixture was put into a 150-mL autoclave for hydrothermal reaction (200 °C, 12 h). The subsequent processing steps were the same as in 2.2 and 2.3.

Citric acid, ethylenediamine and calcium chloride were mixed in 100 mL deionized water at a molar ratio of 1:1:0, 1:1:1, 1:1:2, 1:1:3, 1:1:4, 1:1:5, for the hydrothermal reaction (200 °C, 4 h). The subsequent steps were the same as above.

Citric acid, ethylenediamine and calcium sulfate were mixed in 100 mL deionized water at a molar ratio of 1:1:0, 1:1:1, 1:1:2, 1:1:3, 1:1:4, 1:1:5, for the hydrothermal reaction (200 °C, 4 h). The subsequent steps were the same as above.

The DFT was calculated by the VASP code [27]. The exchange–correlation was processed by the Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof functional [28], and the expansion of the electronic eigenfunctions was characterized by the projectoraugmented-wave pseudopotential [29]. The long-range interactions were characterized by DFT-D method [30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural characterization of ESCDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Before the drying, the ESCDs solution obtained by hydrothermal synthesis was consistent with the characterizations in our previous publication [22], which was brown in daylight and bright blue under 365 nm UV light (as shown in the inset of
Fig S2 ). They were uniformly dispersed in aqueous solution in the form of monodisperse spheres with a particle size of about 20–40 nm (as shown in Fig S1 ). As shown in Fig S1 , the maximum excitation wavelength of the ESCDs solution was located at 325 nm, and the corresponding maximum emission wavelength was located at 405 nm, which was consistent with their blue fluorescence under UV light. The morphological features of ESCDs-based wooden-evaporator (ESCDs-wood) surface and longitudinal cross-sections were characterized by SEM. As shown in Fig. 2
(b)&(d), the balsa wood cross-section contained numerous well-arranged microchannels, which provided great convenience for water transportation. Abundant microscale 3D pore structures of ESCDs layer could be clearly seen (as shown in red dots in Fig. 2 (a)), which provided numerous outlets for water vapor spillage and facilitated efficient interfacial water evaporation. Notably, these pores were not “through” from top to bottom, but had a staggered distribution, i.e., ectopic distribution between layers (as shown by the red arrows in Fig. 2 (c)). Such a pore structure distribution can ensure the escape of water vapor and avoid excessive heat loss at the same time.

The structural features of ESCDs surface were characterized by FTIR and XPS analysis. As shown in Fig. 3
(a), the surface of ESCDs mainly contained abundant oxygen, nitrogen and calcium functional groups. The strong broad peak at 3393.98 cm−1 was originated from the O-H stretching vibration, which indicated the presence of abundant hydrophilic groups on the surface of ESCDs [9]. The sharp peak at 2969.91 cm−1 was corresponds to the C-H stretching vibration. The strong spike at 1590.32 cm−1 was corresponds to the C = C stretching vibration, which was consistent with the graphite core structure of the ESCDs. The spike at 1417.80 cm−1 was originated from the bending vibration of C-OH. The peaks at 1120.92, 1082 and 1047.36 cm−1 were originated from the C-N stretching vibration. The small peaks at 772.08 and 665.82 cm−1 were both originated from the bending vibration of C-H in the benzene ring structure. The sharp peak at 514.82 cm−1 may originate from the vibration of Ca-O [31].

XPS analysis further verified the above FTIR analysis. As depicted in the XPS survey spectrum, the surface of ESCDs was mainly composed of four elements: C, O, N, Ca (Fig. 3 (b)), their contents accounted for 63.92 %, 26.63 %, 6.23 %, and 3.22 %, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s and O1s further revealed that the main chemical forms in the elements C and O included C = O, C-OH and C-O [32] ((Fig. 3 (c)&(d)), which were consistent with the FTIR analysis. The high-resolution spectrum of N1s revealed that nitrogen was doped in ESCDs in the form of pyrrole-N graphite-N, and pyridine-N, and they accounted for 72.80 %, 20.61 %, and 6.60 %, respectively (Fig. 3 (e)). The high-resolution spectrum of Ca2p revealed that calcium was doped in ESCDs in the form of calcium carbonate (CaCO3) (Fig. 3 (f)). Pyrrole-N has a strong interaction force with metals, usually providing multiple binding sites for metal coordination [33], and Ca is the most abundant metallic element in paper mill sludge. The abundant pyrrole nitrogen with may promote N-Ca co-doping in ESCDs by binding to calcium in the synthesis of ESCDs. In summary, ESCD formed Ca-self-doping after a one-pot hydrothermal reaction. In addition, the main functional groups on its surface also included hydroxyl, pyridine nitrogen, and carbonyl groups, which improved the water solubility of ESCD by forming of hydrogen bonding with water [22].

To further verify the conclusion drawn from FTIR and XPS analysis that ESCDs had many hydrophilic structures, the contact angle was utilized to visually investigate the hydrophobicity of the surface of ESCDs. As shown in Fig. 3 (g), when water drops fell on the surface of ESCDs, they spread rapidly on the surface (within 0.69 s), and the contact angle changed from 58.06°/62.29° to 31.73°/31.37° from the moment of contact to stabilization, showing obvious and excellent hydrophilicity.

In conclusion, in terms of the morphological structure, ESCDs-wood had numerous well-arranged micropore channels for water transport in the longitudinal direction, and the ESCDs layer had abundant pore structures to facilitate water vapor spillage. In terms of the chemical structure, ESCDs (i.e., the solar-thermal conversion layer) contained abundant hydrophilic groups, which were favorable for binding with water and thus improve the efficiency of water transport.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solar-thermal conversion and water evaporation of ESCD
ce:section.ce:para:
The light absorption ability of ESCDs in the UV–Vis-NIR band (200 nm-1400 nm) was shown in Fig. 4
(a). ESCDs had a wide range of light absorption, from UV to visible to near-infrared light regions. Among them, the light absorptions in the UV–visible region (200 nm-1000 nm) and the near-infrared region (1500 nm-2500 nm) were at a high level. The solar-thermal conversion of ESCDs was evaluated by monitoring the surface temperature change of ESCDs-wood. Under 1sun illumination (1Kw/m2), the surface temperature of ESCDs-wood rapidly heated up from 15.6 °C (room temperature) to 51.6 °C within 10 min and stabilized at 53.5 °C within 20 to 60 min (Fig. 4 (b)). Compared to pristine balsa wood (heated up to 24 °C under 1 sun) (Fig. 4 (b)), coated ESCDs significantly increased the surface temperature and effectively improved its solar-thermal conversion capacity. Continuing to increase the light intensity revealed a more significant heating effect of ESCDs. The surface temperature of ESCDs-wood rapidly heated up to 87.1 °C under 2 sun illumination, and heated up to 122.8 °C under 4 sun illumination (Fig. 4 (d)). As shown in Table 1 , the STCE of ESCDs was 137.58 % under 1 sun illumination, which achieved ultra-efficient solar-thermal conversion. To the best of our knowledge, this result is higher than all the STCEs reported in previous studies so far (Table 2 ). Therefore, ESCDs had strong sunlight capturing ability and outstanding solar-thermal conversion capacity, which was highly promising green and efficient STCMs.

Furthermore, the water evaporation performance of ESCDs-wood was evaluated. During the simulated solar illumination, the ESCDs-wood evaporation system showed the largest mass changes compared to the pristine balsa wood evaporation system (Fig. 4 (c)), and the evaporation rate of ESCDs-wood was calculated to be 2.94 kg m-2h−1, which was 1.44 times higher than that of the pristine wood (Table 1). The water evaporation effect of pristine balsa wood itself was considerable, and the coating of ESCDs significantly enhanced the evaporation performance of balsa wood, which was consistent with its superior solar-thermal conversion performance. Further increasing the intensity of solar illumination, the evaporation rate of ESCDs-wood was 4.48 kg m-2h−1 at 2 sun and up to 11.52 kg m-2h−1 at 4 sun (Fig. 4 (f)). Therefore, ESCDs-wood system had excellent water evaporation efficiency, which was a highly promising and efficient simple water evaporation system.

In addition, the cyclic experiments of ESCDs-wood were conducted for 20 cycles to investigate the long-term stability of ESCDs-wood in terms of water evaporation and solar-thermal conversion performance. As shown in Fig. S3 , the water evaporation performance of ESCDs-wood showed a decreasing trend with the number of cycles. When cycling to the 20th time, the water evaporation rate of ESCDs-wood was 2.50 kg m-2h−1, which was 85.03 % of the water evaporation rate obtained in the initial experiment. Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. S4 , the solar-thermal conversion performance of ESCDs maintained the overall conversion rate at a high level (greater than 115 %) although it gradually decreased over the 20 cycles, and the solar-thermal conversion efficiency at the 20th cycle was 84.21 % of the initial experiment. In summary, the water evaporation performance as well as the solar-thermal conversion performance of ESCDs-wood maintained at a high level over multiple cycles. Therefore, ESCDs-wood has high stability and potential for long-term cycling.

The solar-thermal conversion performance and the water evaporation of CDs-wood assembled with different chemical derived-CDs was further evaluated. Notably, these two CDs (i.e., CD2 and CD3) are the classical CDs that have been widely studied in the fields of CDs. As shown in Fig. 5
(a), all three CDs-woods were heated up within 10 min, and the stable surface temperature of ESCDs-wood was much higher than that of CD2-wood (27.0 °C) and CD3-wood (45.2 °C). The STCE of ESCDs was 1.69 times higher than that of CD2 (81.56 %) and 1.80 times higher than that of CD3 (76.45 %) (Table 1). As shown in Fig. 5 (b), the mass changes of ESCDs-wood remained higher compared to the water evaporation system of CD-wood assembled by chemical-derived CDs, and the evaporation rate of ESCDs-wood was 1.35 times higher than that of CD2-wood and 1.44 times higher than that of CD2-wood (Table 1). The water evaporation rates of CD2-wood and CD3-wood were similar to that of the pristine balsa wood, i.e., the chemical derived CDs coating almost had no effect on the water evaporation performance enhancement of balsa wood ( Table 1 ).

Furthermore, the solar-thermal conversion and water evaporation performance of other SIWEs in previous studies were compared. As shown in Fig. 5 (c), 41 SIWEs, including SIWEs constructed from six types of STCMs, including carbon-based materials, CDs, semiconductors, polymers, waste/biomass derivatives, and plasma, were listed and compared.

The STCE of most STCMs were in the range of 70 %-100 %, and the water evaporation rates for corresponding evaporators (at 1 sun) were in the range of 0.47–4.00 kg m-2h−1. Among 41 SIWEs, only the CDs/Fluorophores hybrid constructed by Nhat Hang et al [26] had an STCE of more than 100 %, reaching 133.9 %. Therefore, for water evaporation rate and STCE, the water evaporation rate of the ESCDs-wood (2.94 kg m-2h−1) exceeded 83.3 % of the water evaporation rates of the previous studies (41 SIWEs). Meanwhile, the STCE of the ESCDs-wood was the highest among the above 42 SIWEs. To the best of our knowledge, it was also the highest of all existing SIWEs.

The cost of ESCDs from preparations to subsequent interfacial water evaporation applications were evaluated in this study, and compared with chemical-derived CDs. It included the market prices of raw materials and chemicals, and water evaporation cost (based on per m2). The market price of excess sludge disposal cost is 280 RMB per ton [71], and this part can be considered as a raw material subsidy for the ESCDs preparation. In contrast, all raw materials for the preparation of chemical-derived CDs (CD2 and CD3) need to be purchased, while ESCDs used only excess sludge, water, and balsa wood from preparation to water evaporation application, and no chemicals were used throughout, which was the main difference in cost between CD2-wood/CD3-wood and ESCDs. As shown in Table 3 , CD2-wood cost 396.16 RMB to complete 1 m2 water evaporation, CD3-wood cost 395.52 RMB to complete 1 m2 water evaporation, while ESCDs-wood cost only 64 RMB per square meter of water evaporation, which was about 1/6 of the cost of CD2-wood or CD3-wood. Therefore, the preparation of ESCDs from excess sludge for interfacial water evaporation achieved chemical-free and easy-operation of the whole process, which was a promising simple, green, and low-cost new strategy.

Since solar-thermal conversion is the core process of SIWE, the thermal stability of solar-thermal conversion layers needs to be ensured for the long-term stability of the system. Therefore, the thermal stability of ESCDs, CD2, and CD3 was analyzed and compared by thermogravimetric characterization. As shown in Fig. S5 , the residual mass of the CD3 was only 3.32 % at 800 ℃, while the residual mass of the CD2 was 32.81 % at 800 ℃. In contrast, the residual mass of the ESCD was still as high as 43.03 % at 800 ℃, which was obviously higher the two classic chemical-derived CDs. Moreover, the residual mass was as high as 99.06 % when the thermogravimetric analysis temperature was 54 °C (the surface temperature at which ESCDs undergo solar-thermal conversion). Therefore, ESCDs exhibited excellent thermal stability, which was advantageous in long-term solar-thermal conversion.

In the future applications, due to the advantages of low cost, environmental friendliness, easy preparation, and easy floating, ESCDs wood can be placed at various water environment interfaces such as seawater, sewage, and river water for solar water evaporation. Meanwhile, using the inherent functional groups on the surface of ESCDs (e.g., carbonyl, which can be excited by visible light to produce single-linear oxygen, which has a degrading effect on pollutants) [22] or introducing specific functional groups or other functional materials, pollutants such as heavy metals, bacteria and volatile organic compounds in water can be removed to achieve synergistic water purification by water evaporation. In addition, given the unique photovoltaic conversion properties of CDs themselves [8], ESCDs-wood, if designed and optimized, may achieve synergistic electricity generation by water evaporation. Therefore, ESCDs-wood was expected to achieve synergistic water purification/simultaneous electricity generation by water evaporation for a wide range of water bodies, which has a high potential for application in practice.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced effect of Ca-doping on solar-thermal conversion
ce:section.ce:para: According to the above discussion, it was hypothesized that Ca-doping was an important factor affecting the efficient solar-thermal conversion of ESCDs. Therefore, to further investigate the effect of Ca-doping on the solar-thermal conversion performance of CDs, we conducted three sets of validation experiments as follows:

Firstly, the ordinary municipal excess sludge (MS) was used to simulate the paper mill sludge without Ca, and then added different levels of externally added Ca sources (CaCl2). As shown in Fig. 6
(a)&(b), When no Ca was added, the surface temperature of MSCDs increased and stabilized from an initial 28.9 °C to 45.3 °C after one hour of 1 sun illumination. However, the addition of Ca resulted in the formation of Ca-doped MSCDs that increased and stabilized their surface temperatures to 51.2–55.4 °C after one hour of 1 sun illumination, which was 1.13–1.22 times higher than the MSCDs without Ca-doping. Meanwhile, with the gradual increase in the mass ratio of CaCl2 to municipal sludge (from 4:1 to 1:1), the solar-thermal conversion temperatures of the corresponding MSCDs showed a monotonically increasing trend, in which the solar-thermal conversion temperatures reached a maximum when the mass ratio of CaCl2 to municipal sludge was 4:3.

The above results indicated to some extent that Ca-doping may be able to have an enhancing effect on the photothermal performance of CDs. However, this could also be caused by the chlorine in the CaCl2 or the other complex components of the municipal sludge. Therefore, to further rule out potential influences, we selected a basic chemical-derived CDs (CD3, synthesized from citric acid and ethylenediamine) and doped different calcium sources (calcium chloride and calcium sulfate) for subsequent validation experiments.

As shown in Fig. 6 (c)&(d), when CD3 was not doped with CaCl2, the surface temperature of CD3 increased from 14.3 °C to 45.2 °C after 1 h under 1 sun irradiation. In contrast, when CD3 was doped with CaCl2, the surface temperature of CD3 increased significantly. When the dosing ratio of citric acid, ethylenediamine, and CaCl2 increased from 1:1:1 to 1:1:5, the corresponding maximum surface temperature of CD3 also increased gradually. Among them, when the ratio was 1:1:5, the maximum temperature of the CD3 surface was as high as 63.3 °C, which was 1.4 times higher than the maximum temperature of the surface of the undoped CD3 with CaCl2. Therefore, the amount of Ca-doping was positively correlated with the solar-thermal conversion efficiency of CD3, and CaCl2-doping effectively enhanced the solar-thermal conversion performance of CD3.

To further exclude the effect of chlorine atoms on CD3, we performed subsequent experiments with calcium sulfate doped CD3. As shown in Fig. 6 (c)&(d), when CD3 was not doped with CaSO4, the surface temperature of CD3 increased from 15.1 °C to 44.5 °C after 1 h under 1 sun irradiation. In contrast, when CD3 was doped with CaSO4, the surface temperature of CD3 increased significantly. When the dosage ratio of citric acid, ethylenediamine, and CaSO4 was increased from 1:1:1 to 1:1:5, the corresponding maximum surface temperature of CD3 increased gradually from 53.4 °C to 60.6 °C. Therefore, the amount of Ca-doping was positively correlated with the solar-thermal conversion efficiency of CD3, and CaSO4-doping effectively enhanced the solar-thermal conversion performance of CD3.
In conclusion, Ca-doping has been proved to be a key factor in in improving the photothermal performance of CDs, and Ca-doping can effectively improve the solar-thermal conversion performance of CDs.

In addition, thermogravimetric analysis was investigated to evaluate the effect of Ca-doping on the thermal stability of CDs. As shown in Fig. S6
(a), when no Ca was added, the residual mass of the corresponding MSCDs was only 25.39 % at 800 ℃. When the mass ratio of CaCl2 to MS mass was 1:1, the residual mass of the corresponding MSCDs was still 61.18 % at 800 ℃, and the residual mass reached as high as 94.50 % at 100 ℃. It indicated that Ca-doping effectively improved the thermal stability of MSCDs. As shown in Fig. S6
(b), when no Ca was added, the residual mass of the corresponding CD3 was only 3.32 % at 800 ℃. With the increase of the dosing ratio of citric acid, ethylenediamine and CaCl2 from 1:1:1 to 1:1:5, the thermal stability of the corresponding CD3 was effectively improved. When the ratio was 1:1:5, the mass residual rate of CD3 was as high as 61.67 % at 800 °C, which was 18.6 times higher than the CD3 without Ca-doping. Therefore, Ca-doping effectively enhanced the thermal stability of CDs, which will facilitate the long-term stability of CDs in solar-thermal energy conversion applications.

In conclusion, Ca-doping has been shown to be effective in improving the solar-thermal conversion performance and thermal stability of CDs, which not only explained the ultra-high solar-thermal conversion efficiency of ESCDs, but also innovatively revealed the functionalization of Ca-doping for CDs for the first time.

Notably, the high-Ca property of paper mill sludge feedstock could provide a natural advantage for CDs to form Ca-doping. Therefore, solar interfacial water evaporation based on the solar-thermal conversion was an application strategy that can bring out the advantages of ESCDs characterized by Ca-doping.

The fundamental solar-thermal conversion mechanism of ESCDs was based on the thermal vibration in molecules. Specifically, Under the solar radiation, the electrons in the ESCDs were excited from the HOMO) to LUMO, the excited electrons returned to the ground state through the electron coupling relaxation in order to generate thermal energy. Therefore, the smaller the bandgap of ESCDs, the higher the corresponding solar-thermal conversion efficiency.

As shown in Fig. 7 , with the increase of Ca-doping concentration, the peak signal corresponding to C = C (1621 cm−1) was gradually enhanced, representing the increase of sp2 hybridized carbon structure, i.e., the increase of conjugated π-bonds. It effectively decreased the energy gap between HOMO and LUMO, and further improved the efficiency of solar-thermal conversion. In the meanwhile, the peak signals corresponding to hydroxyl group (3383 cm−1), C-N (1405 cm−1), and Ca-O (494 cm−1) were also obviously enhanced with the increase of Ca-doping concentration. Combined with the results of the XPS analysis of Ca in Fig. 3, the Ca-O bond corresponded to the structure of CaCO3. Therefore, the main surface groups of ESCDs were hydroxyl group, C-N, and CaCO3.

The DFT was used to further calculate the effect of these surface groups on the charge density distribution of ESCD to investigate their influence on the solar-thermal conversion performance of ESCD. As illustrated in Fig. S7 , the energy gap between HOMO (0 eV) and LUMO (2.76 eV) for CDs undoped with surface groups was 2.76 eV, while the energy gap between HOMO (0.18 eV) and LUMO (2.53 eV) for CDs doped with hydroxyl and pyridine nitrogen was 2.35 eV. It indicated that doping hydroxyl, and pyridinic nitrogen effectively reduced the energy gap between HOMO and LUMO of CDs.

As revealed in Section 3.5 , Ca-doping was proved to be effective in improving the solar-thermal conversion performance of CDs. Therefore, the effect of Ca-doping on the CD energy gap was further characterized. As shown in Fig. 8 , the energy gap between HOMO (0.24 eV) and LUMO (2.37 eV) for CDs doped with CaCO3 (C atom: Ca atom ratios was 5:1), hydroxyl and pyridine nitrogen was 2.13 eV, which was obviously lower than that of CDs without Ca-doping. Furthermore, with a gradual increase in the Ca-doping ratio (C: Ca atomic ratio from 5:1 to 1:1), the energy gap between HOMO and LUMO of the CDs gradually decreased from 2.13 eV to 1.42 eV. Therefore, Ca-doping reduced the energy bands of CDs and the doping amount was negatively correlated with the energy gaps of CDs, which in turn can improve the solar-thermal conversion efficiency of CDs. Possibly, Ca and O atoms in CaCO3 doped on the surface of CDs have positive and negative charges interacted with the π-electrons on the inner core of the CDs, which may cause charge transfer with the CDs. This charge transfer can lead to the rearrangement of the electronic states, which may reduce the energy gaps of the CDs. Meanwhile, the Ca-doping may introduce new energy levels into the energy gap structure of the CDs core. These additional energy levels can cross the energy gap structure of the CDs core and form new electronic states, affecting the electronic structure and energy gap of the CDs and thus reducing its energy gaps.

In conclusion, the major functional groups doped on the surface of ESCDs, including hydroxyl groups, pyridinic-N, and CaCO3, were shown to reduce the energy gap of ESCDs. Among them, Ca-doping was the key structure for tuning the photovoltaic structure of ESCDs, and the energy gaps of ESCDs reduced with the increase of Ca-doping. Meanwhile, the sp2 structure in ESCDs increased with higher Ca-doping level, which in turn lead to the decrease of the energy gap of the ESCDs. Together, the above reasons lead to a lower band gap of ESCDs, which in turn effectively improved the solar-thermal conversion performance of ESCDs.

In this study, paper mill sludge (PMS) was converted into functional CDs for the construction of an innovative cost-effectiveness, high performance, sustainability, chemical-free, and eco-friendly wooden-solar interfacial evaporator (ESCDs-wood). Under 1 sun illumination, ESCDs showed super-efficient light capture capability with an ultra-high solar-thermal conversion efficiency of 137.58 %. ESCDs-wood had excellent water evaporation efficiency, which corresponded to 2.94 kg m-2h−1 at 1 sun, 4.48 kg m-2h−1 at 2 suns, and 11.52 kg m-2h−1 at 4 suns. The ESCDs-wood exhibited long-term stability, its solar-thermal conversion efficiency and water evaporation rate remained at a high level of 115.37 % and 2.50 kg m-2h−1, respectively, after 20 cycles. The economic analysis showed that the ESCDs-wood cost only $9 to evaporate 1 m2 of water, indicating its advantage of low cost and economically feasible for large-scale production. The Ca-doping played a vital role in enhancing the solar-thermal conversion performance and thermal stability of the ESCDs, and the high-Ca property of paper mill sludge provided a natural advantage for the Ca-in situ-functionalization of ESCDs. The exact role of Ca doping in improving CD photothermal conversion performance was verified and revealed using different kinds of CDs, including municipal sludge-derived carbon dots and chemical-derived CDs combined with different kinds of calcium salts. The DFT simulations indicated that Ca-doping probably reduced the energy required for molecular thermal vibration by effectively tuning the energy gap of ESCDs, which in turn improved the solar-thermal conversion efficiency. Therefore, this work successfully converted waste to high value-added photothermal materials, which offered a new strategy for waste utilization. Moreover, this work also broke the knowledge gap of the underlying mechanism between the PMS properties and the photothermal properties of its derived CDs, and innovatively revealed the functionalization mechanism of Ca-doping for CDs, which offered new perspective for the targeted regulation and design of CDs.

Yutong Han: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation. Jingjing Zheng: Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Lecheng Wei: Software, Methodology. Liang Zhu: Funding acquisition.
